summary: Spolsky uses the analogy of a perpetually packed Manhattan restaurant called Isabella's to argue that great software succeeds not through brilliant features but through being 'deeply debugged.' Isabella's thrives despite mediocre food because in ten years of patronage, nothing ever went wrong — every edge case in the dining experience was handled. He extends this to software development, arguing that bug tracking databases are not just scheduling tools but the essential mechanism that makes great software possible. By systematically capturing and fixing every flaw, misunderstanding, and edge case, software evolves from merely functional to truly reliable.
key_points: A restaurant can thrive despite mediocre food simply by never giving customers a reason to stop coming back — the same principle applies to software
Great software is defined not by features but by being 'deeply debugged' — handling every edge case, every misunderstanding, every rare scenario
Bug tracking is not merely a convenience tool but a prerequisite for creating great software, because it captures every flaw and improvement opportunity systematically
With millions of users, even one-in-a-million bugs happen constantly — 'In our business, one in a million is next Tuesday'
Reliability — software that never frustrates its users — is a 'magic' emergent property that comes from relentless bug fixing and triage
money_quotes: Isabella's is thoroughly and completely debugged.
Every time somebody is unhappy, that's a bug. Write it down. Figure out what you're going to do about it. Add it to the training manual. Never make the same mistake twice.
Great products are great because they're deeply debugged. Restaurants, software, it's all the same.
In our business, one in a million is next Tuesday.
A bug tracking database is not just a memory aid, or a scheduling tool. It doesn't make it easier to produce great software, it makes it possible to create great software.
Bug tracking software captures the cosmic rays that cause the genetic mutations that make your software evolve into something superior.
Suddenly it becomes reliable. Reliable, meaning, it never screws up. It never makes its users angry. It never makes its customers wish they had purchased something else.
key_insight: Great software, like a great restaurant, succeeds not through brilliance but through relentless debugging of every edge case until it becomes so reliable that users never have a reason to leave.
